did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used an ensemble deep learning algorithm combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) with a Stacking meta-learner (Random Forest) to classify skin cancer types (Melanoma and Basal Cell Carcinoma) using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing and validating an ensemble deep learning algorithm (CNN and LSTM with Stacking) for skin cancer detection using the HAM10000 dataset. It includes a methodology section, hyperparameter tuning experiments, and reports new performance metrics (e.g., 97.97% accuracy), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Study of the Ensemble Deep Learning Algorithm to Detect Image-Based Skin Cancer Melanoma and Basal Cell Carcinoma
journal: 2024 12th International Conference on Information and Communication Technology (ICoICT)
brief_summary: This study developed an ensemble deep learning algorithm combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) with a Random Forest meta-learner to classify skin cancer types, specifically Melanoma and Basal Cell Carcinoma. The model was trained and evaluated using the HAM10000 dataset, which was preprocessed with Gaussian filtering and feature extraction techniques including Hue Saturation Value (HSV) and Gray Level Co-Occurrence Matrix (GLCM). The researchers compared two scenarios: models trained with and without hyperparameter tuning. The results demonstrated that the stacking method with hyperparameter tuning achieved superior performance, reaching an accuracy, precision, and F1-score of 97.97%, along with a sensitivity of 97.22% and specificity of 97.62%. These findings indicate that the proposed ensemble approach significantly improves detection performance compared to existing methods.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two primary classes selected from the HAM10000 dataset for the classification task. The dataset contained 1188 original data points, with 575 used in this study.
One of the two primary classes selected from the HAM10000 dataset for the classification task. The dataset contained 589 original data points, with 575 used in this study.
A third class included in the study's dataset, obtained from Telkom University healthy students, containing 575 data points. Used as a comparator to melanoma and BCC.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer types (Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Normal Skin)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs a multi-class classification task to distinguish between three discrete diagnostic categories: Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), and Normal Skin. The model predicts class labels using an ensemble deep learning algorithm (CNN and LSTM with Stacking meta-learner). Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall (sensitivity), specificity, and F1-score, with a reported accuracy of 97.97% on the test set.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of skin lesion images sourced from Kaggle (referenced as 'Skin cancer MNIST HAM10000 dataset'). The study utilized a subset of this dataset, selecting only two classes: melanoma and basal cell carcinoma. The original HAM10000 contains seven classes, but only these two were used for this specific study.

use.training: Used for training the CNN and LSTM base learners in the ensemble model.
use.validation: Used for validation (specifically, the best model achieved an average validation score of 97%).
use.testing: Used for testing the final ensemble model.

datasets_used.name: Telkom University Healthy Student Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset consisting of images of normal skin conditions obtained directly from healthy students at Telkom University.

use.training: Used for training the model as the 'normal skin' class.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To ensure the model is well-trained and generalizes well to test data by producing independent models at each training and testing iteration.
To find the optimal combination of values for hyperparameters (epoch and batch size) to improve model performance.
To visualize classification outcomes (TP, TN, FP, FN) and calculate performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, and F1-score.
To report the variability of mean accuracy across different hyperparameter combinations during model training.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made based on descriptive statistics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score) and standard deviation of mean accuracy across hyperparameter combinations.

HAM10000 Dataset (Melanoma and Basal Cel.dataset_description: Total 1725 data points (575 per class: Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Normal Skin). 1380 used for training, 345 used for testing.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble Deep Learning Algorithm combining Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) with Stacking meta-learner (Random Forest)

Attribute Selected Classifier with Color Layout Filter

conclusions: The proposed ensemble deep learning algorithm combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) with a Random Forest meta-learner (Stacking) achieved superior performance for skin cancer detection compared to existing methods.

Implementing hyperparameter tuning significantly enhanced the model's performance, with the best model (Model 1) achieving an accuracy, precision, and F1-score of 97.97%, a sensitivity of 97.22%, and a specificity of 97.62%.

The model with hyperparameter tuning outperformed the model without tuning in terms of accuracy, precision, and F1-score, although it showed slightly lower sensitivity and specificity compared to the best untuned model.

The confusion matrix analysis indicated excellent performance with no errors in distinguishing between melanoma and normal conditions, but identified one error in distinguishing between basal cell carcinoma and normal conditions.

The proposed method achieved higher accuracy (97.97%) than baseline studies using Ensemble Learning with Attribute Selected Classifier (90.96%), Ensemble VGG/Caps-Net/ResNet (93.50%), and Combined VGG 16/LSTM (90.89%).
important_remarks: The study uses a highly imbalanced and non-standard dataset composition: it combines a subset of the HAM10000 dataset (575 melanoma, 575 BCC) with a private, non-public dataset of 575 normal skin images collected from healthy Telkom University students, limiting generalizability and external validation.
The dataset size is very small (total 1,725 images, with only 345 for testing), which increases the risk of overfitting and reduces the statistical power of the results.

The paper does not report any formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) to validate the superiority of the proposed method over baselines; comparisons are based solely on descriptive accuracy metrics.
There is a notable discrepancy in the reported sensitivity for the best model: the abstract and results section state 97.22%, while the discussion section incorrectly states 92.22%.

The model achieved only one error in distinguishing Basal Cell Carcinoma from Normal Skin in the test set, which may indicate data leakage or insufficient difficulty in the test split given the small sample size.
The study lacks external validation on independent, diverse clinical datasets, relying instead on internal cross-validation and a single held-out test set from a limited source.
Hyperparameter tuning was performed using Grid Search, but the paper does not specify the computational cost or time required for this process, which is relevant for deployment feasibility.
